data.title: One-Punch Man backlash exposes cracks in Japan and South Korea’s soft power
data.description:
When online outrage torpedoed the latest episode of One‑Punch Man, few saw past the animation flaws. But what followed exposed a creative industry buckling under impossible expectations.
The sixth episode of the long-awaited third season of the Japanese superhero saga – once praised for its sharp humour and kinetic style – stumbled onto screens under the weight of its own hype in October.
Viewers flooded social media with complaints about its uneven pacing and “rushed” storytelling. On reviews database IMDb, the episode’s audience rating collapsed to 1.4, a brutal comedown for a franchise accustomed to critical acclaim that had usually scored between 7.3 and 9.5.
Days later, the show’s director Shinpei Nagai deleted his social media accounts after posting a message that said the backlash had become too much to bear.
“Honestly, this is taking a toll on my mental health, and it only brings negatives to the work, the staff and the original creators,” he wrote in Japanese.
The sudden implosion of goodwill for One-Punch Man rippled far beyond its immediate fandom. For many inside Japan and South Korea’s creative circles, the uproar captured how social media scrutiny, industrial workloads and fan expectations are converging into a near-unliveable pressure cooker.

Built on burnout
Creative fatigue is inevitable in long-running projects, according to Kim So-won, a research professor at South Korea’s Kyung Hee University who studies the region’s comic industries.
“There are limits in coming up with ideas for a series if only one person is in charge,” she told This Week in Asia, adding that keeping ideas fresh was the real challenge.
Those demands have only intensified as anime and webtoons – the name for South Korea’s digital comics – have evolved into global cultural exports worth billions.
Yet behind the screens, many artists describe an emotional and physical grind that few outsiders see.
In Japan, where manga and anime are pillars of soft power, production cycles can stretch over years while budgets thin and deadlines shrink.
One‑Punch Man, whose first season debuted a decade ago, has switched studios and directors multiple times, producing noticeable stylistic shifts and production challenges. The six-year hiatus between seasons two and three supercharged fan expectations and left little margin for error when the new episodes resumed.

“There’s immense pressure on the artists to come up with new material that satisfies the fan base,” said Yang Ji-hoon, an associate research fellow at the Korea Culture and Tourism Institute.
“The rate of success is low, while disappointment from fans that comes after is tremendous.”
South Korea’s webtoon industry faces similar strains. Valued at about 2 trillion won (US$1.36 billion), the sector has become a cornerstone of K-culture exports. Platforms such as Naver and Kakao are investing aggressively overseas, from Marvel collaborations to Star Wars tie-ins for US audiences.
But rapid growth has taken its toll. Artists report working an average of nearly six days a week, often for more than nine hours a day. Roughly one in 10 said they had faced copyright disputes, a survey last year by South Korea’s culture ministry found.
A 2024 white paper by the government’s Korea Creative Content Agency found that while the webtoon market had expanded, average annual artist earnings fell by more than 22 million won (US$15,000).

The dopamine war
Veteran webtoonist Kwak Baek-soo, creator of Gaus Electronics and other hit titles, said conditions had improved since a wave of protests a decade ago, with new contracts giving artists a larger share of digital royalties – as much as 50 per cent in some cases, compared with about 10 per cent for many Japanese manga creators.
Yet he warned that competition for studio jobs had never been fiercer.
“It’s hard to get a job in these studios these days,” Kwak said. “On top of employment struggles, we have to compete with short reels and YouTube, which I think is at an advantage when providing a shot of dopamine to audiences.”
Feeding the relentless attention economy has deep human consequences. Last year’s suicide of manga artist Hinako Ashihara, who days earlier had bitterly criticised a television adaptation of her work Sexy Tanaka-san, exposed the darker side of Japan’s creative hierarchy.
There’s immense pressure on the artists to come up with new material that satisfies the fan base
Yang Ji-hoon, cultural policy researcher
Fellow artists and industry observers said her death reflected a wider sense of exhaustion and invisibility among creators who felt squeezed by commercialisation. In South Korea, a recent study on working conditions in the industry found that artists regularly faced “high labour intensity, limited job discretion and a notable prevalence of depressive symptoms”.
“In the industry, there’s a perception that being a manga artist is a gruelling occupation with long work hours and low wages,” said Kim of Kyung Hee University. “And there is no guarantee that you will become a famous artist.”
As anime and webtoons become ever more mainstream, they carry with them a paradox that no algorithm can resolve: the more connected fans and creators feel, the more exposed the creative process and its human limits become.
If you have suicidal thoughts or know someone who is experiencing them, help is available. In Hong Kong, you can dial 18111 for the government-run Mental Health Support Hotline. You can also call +852 2896 0000 for The Samaritans or +852 2382 0000 for Suicide Prevention Services. In the US, call or text 988 or chat at 988lifeline.org for the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. For a list of other nations’ helplines, see this page.

data.title: Is China shifting its policy and accepting North Korea as a nuclear-armed state?
data.description:
Beijing’s omission of the term “denuclearisation of the Korean peninsula” from a new white paper suggested its “tacit acceptance” of North Korea as a nuclear-armed state while prioritising its strategic competition with Washington, analysts said.
Last week, China released a white paper on the country’s arms control, disarmament and non-proliferation, outlining its policies on national defence and nuclear policy and updating a previous paper from 2005.
One notable shift in Beijing’s position on the Korean peninsula was its omission of its traditional support for the peninsula’s denuclearisation within the paper’s overall “non-proliferation” clause.
“With regard to the Korean peninsula issue, China adopts an impartial stance and adheres to the right approach of always working for the peace, stability and prosperity of the peninsula and the resolution of the Korean peninsula issue through political means,” according to the document.
“China calls on relevant parties to desist from an approach based on aggressive deterrence and coercion, restart dialogue and negotiations, and play a constructive role in resolving the Korean peninsula issue through political means and realising lasting peace and stability in the peninsula.”
The omission of the term “denuclearisation” suggested Beijing had made a significant departure from its previous position, according to analysts.


In its preceding arms control and non-proliferation white paper released in 2005, Beijing stated that “China supports denuclearisation of the Korean peninsula”.
Another white paper on China’s policies on Asia-Pacific security cooperation in 2017 said Beijing was “committed to the denuclearisation of the [Korean] peninsula, its peace and stability, and settlement of the issue through dialogue and consultation”.
Zhao Tong, senior fellow at the Washington-based Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, said Beijing in the past year and a half had “clearly shifted away” from mentioning “denuclearisation” of the Korean peninsula in its official statements and policy documents.
“If Beijing can no longer bring itself to utter the phrase ‘denuclearisation of the Korean peninsula’, that is, in effect, tacit acceptance of a nuclear-armed North Korea,” Zhao added.
“Under repeated pressure from Pyongyang, Beijing now appears to have decided to accommodate North Korea’s demand to drop the nuclear issue, an issue that had repeatedly complicated bilateral relations in recent years.”
Beijing’s apparent policy shift on the Korean peninsula follows its recently warming ties with Pyongyang.
In September, North Korean leader Kim Jong-un attended China’s large-scale military parade in Beijing and held bilateral summits with Chinese President Xi Jinping and Russian President Vladimir Putin.
In their talks after the parade, Xi and Kim pledged to “strengthen strategic coordination in international and regional affairs to safeguard common interests”, according to Chinese state media.
That marked a contrast from Xi’s last visit to Pyongyang in 2019, when he told Kim that China was willing to play a “positive and constructive role in achieving denuclearisation of the Korean peninsula and long-term stability in the region”.

Zhao said Beijing’s refraining from publicly invoking denuclearisation reflected a “broader recalibration” prioritising strategic competition with the United States.
But he believed the approach risked “inevitably” weakening Beijing’s capacity to press Pyongyang on nuclear restraint.
“Beijing seems to have concluded that keeping North Korea close and preserving China’s geopolitical leverage on the peninsula matters more than cooperating with Washington to curb Pyongyang’s nuclear expansion,” Zhao said.
North Korea has advanced its nuclear programme in recent years, especially since Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022. Pyongyang has supplied Moscow with weapons and, more recently, troops in the armed conflict in exchange for Russian aid and weapons technology.
At a military parade in Pyongyang in October, North Korea rolled out its latest solid-fuelled Hwasong-20 intercontinental-range ballistic missiles (ICBMs), reportedly with technological help from Russia. The event was attended by Premier Li Qiang, the first Chinese premier to visit the country in 16 years.
Meanwhile, Kim has repeatedly rejected US President Donald Trump and South Korean President Lee Jae-myung’s proposals to resume denuclearisation dialogue, insisting that recognition of North Korea as a nuclear-armed state must be a condition of any talks.
Timothy Heath, a senior international defence researcher at the US-based Rand Corporation, said Beijing appeared to have adopted a “pragmatic approach” by accepting a nuclear-armed North Korea as a reality.
“China’s shift is probably driven in part by Russia’s active support for the DPRK’s nuclear weapons programme,” said Heath, referring to North Korea by the initials of its official name.
Moscow’s support made it easier for Pyongyang to build its nuclear weapons inventory, he added, and “puts pressure on China to at least maintain a neutral stance on the issue to maintain stable ties with North Korea and Russia”.


Patrick Cronin, the Asia-Pacific security chair at the Washington-based think tank Hudson Institute, said China wanted to project itself as the “most principled defender of denuclearisation” on its own terms.
Cronin said China’s posture served as “a subtle protest” against the US’ strengthening of extended deterrence alongside South Korea and Japan, and as a way to “deflect growing international pressure to rein in Pyongyang’s expanding nuclear programme”.
Extended deterrence refers to the US policy of assuring its allies and partners of deterrence against attacks from adversaries by providing a security umbrella, including the use of nuclear arsenals.
Washington has used the policy for decades to deter allies such as South Korea and Japan from developing their own nuclear arsenals.
The strategy has helped maintain a non-proliferation mechanism in the Asia-Pacific region, despite China and North Korea’s increasing nuclear capabilities.
The new white paper said China “firmly opposes” nuclear sharing, extended deterrence and other arrangements that “undermine” the international nuclear non-proliferation mechanism. It urged relevant countries to stop deploying nuclear weapons abroad.
Without identifying the US by name, the paper stated that a “certain country” sought “absolute strategic superiority” by constantly expanding its armaments, strengthening combat readiness and provoking bloc confrontation.
Heath said China’s “acquiescence” to North Korea as a nuclear-armed power meant there was a risk that America’s regional allies would seek protection from those weapons either through the US’ extended nuclear deterrence or by developing their own.

Washington could respond by modernising and upgrading its nuclear weapons capabilities such that they could protect America’s allies, which “may mean more US nuclear bombs in the Indo-Pacific region”, he continued.
“China’s acceptance of North Korea as a nuclear-armed power may buy stability” in ties between Beijing and Pyongyang, Heath added, but it was likely to “worsen the overall security situation in Asia”.
In a parallel move, Washington has also recently shifted away from its traditional approach to seeking a denuclearised Korean peninsula. The change comes amid North Korea’s advances in its nuclear capabilities and closer ties with China and Russia.
In October, the Trump administration granted South Korea approval to build its own nuclear-powered submarine and start updating their bilateral atomic energy pact.
This happened after Lee said Seoul needed enhanced submersibility in its naval fleet to track Chinese and North Korean submarines.
Washington’s blessing was seen as a pivot from its decades-long opposition to Seoul securing its own nuclear capabilities, stemming from the concern that developing technologies for nuclear-powered submarines could directly lead to the development of nuclear weapons.
Kang Jun-young, a professor of Chinese studies at the Hankuk University of Foreign Studies in Seoul, called China’s new white paper an “indirect expression” of Beijing’s view that denuclearisation of the Korean peninsula would be “difficult” to achieve, considering both Koreas’ development of nuclear-powered submarines.
“From China’s perspective, on the one hand, the introduction of South Korean nuclear-powered submarines into the Korean peninsula, with US approval, is a sign of the influx of nuclear materials for military use,” Kang said.
“On the other hand, given North Korea’s existing illegal nuclear development and its claim that it is building nuclear-powered submarines, continuing to talk about the denuclearisation of the Korean peninsula may be unreasonable.”

In 2021, the North Korean leader announced that his country planned to build its own nuclear-powered submarines.
Despite scepticism about North Korea’s technological capabilities, the plan appeared to be materialising in March when Kim visited a shipyard that was said to be building a “nuclear-powered strategic guided-missile submarine”, according to Pyongyang’s Korean Central News Agency.
As North Korea has made advances, America’s decision supporting South Korea has come as the Trump administration exerts pressure on US allies to spend more on their own defence.
Elbridge Colby, US undersecretary of defence for policy, has said in media interviews that “all options” should be on the table to ensure South Korea’s security, including its nuclear armament in the face of a nuclear build-up in North Korea and China.
And amid Beijing’s stance of both prioritising its strategic competition with Washington and intensifying criticism of the US’ extended deterrence, Zhao described the approach as “seemingly counterintuitive”.
Proliferation pressures were increasingly originating “from Washington’s own allies” while the US nuclear umbrella had long helped contain those risks, he said.
“Yet Beijing’s competition-first orientation has amplified its suspicion that Washington is using extended deterrence as a pretext for expanding forward deployment of US nuclear capabilities in the region to constrain China,” Zhao added.
“The secrecy surrounding Beijing’s ultimate objectives – what end state it envisions and what military purpose a substantially larger arsenal is meant to serve – has put the United States on course to reverse decades of nuclear reductions.”

data.title: Australian TikTokker gifts widowed US cashier retirement with US$1.7 million donation
data.description:
A young man from Australia with a huge social media following has given an 88-year-old US supermarket worker the gift of retirement.
Ed Bambas from Michigan was presented with an oversized cheque for US$1.7 million on Friday – the result of an extraordinary online fundraising drive.
“No, no,” Bambas said, wiping away tears in front of reporters. “Thank you. Oh, my God.”
Sam Weidenhofer, 22, from Melbourne, is using his online influence to spread kindness – and financial support – during a visit to the United States.

@itssozer 88 year old veteran still working.. 😭❤️ (donate in B1O) #love #kind #veteran ♬ original sound - Samuel Weidenhofer

He met Bambas at a Meijer store in Brighton, southeastern Michigan, about a fortnight ago, filming a TikTok video for his 7.7 million followers in which the General Motors retiree explained why he continues to work as he nears 90, following the death of his wife, Joan, after a long illness in 2018.
“I don’t have enough income,” Bambas said in the video.
Weidenhofer responded by launching a GoFundMe appeal, urging people around the world to help Bambas.
“His story is a stark reminder that too many of our seniors, especially veterans, face incredible challenges just to survive,” said Weidenhofer, whose combined online audience exceeds 10 million.
I have to find everybody and say, ‘thank you’
Ed Bambas, 88
The response was extraordinary: more than 15,000 people made donations ranging from US$15 to US$10,000.
“It means a terrible burden,” Bambas jokingly told reporters. “I have to find everybody and say, ‘thank you.’”
Bambas began working at Meijer, a retail chain selling groceries, clothing and other goods, when he was 82.
“I talk to everybody that came through my cashier line because it helped me not become despondent on her loss. … I gave them a piece of my life story,” he said, referring to his wife.
Lexi Wallace, 26, a former regular customer who has since moved away, went on to Weidenhofer’s Facebook page and encouraged him to locate Bambas.
“I thought his name was Bob. He never corrected me,” Wallace said. “I would love going to Meijer to see him.”
Weidenhofer said the windfall will enable Bambas to clear about US$225,000 in debt, with the rest for him to use as he wishes.
“It feels like a dream,” Weidenhofer said.
Bambas now hopes to visit his brother and return to golf. As for work, he’s not quite ready to hang up his lanyard.
“I’ll probably work another month or two and shut things down,” he said.

data.title: Asia is waking up to storms that are stronger – and more polluted
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
A combination of tropical cyclones and heavy monsoon rains is wreaking havoc across South and Southeast Asia, showing the extent of the damage wrought by severe storms. But high winds and flooding are not the only threats.
New research led by a team at the University of Nottingham Ningbo reveals an additional, invisible threat: typhoons have become efficient super-spreaders of microplastic pollution.
Our study, published last month in Environmental Science & Technology, demonstrates that typhoons function like massive conveyor belts, vacuuming microplastics from the ocean and depositing them across land. While Ningbo in Zhejiang province was under the influence of Typhoon Gaemi last year, we measured peak deposition rates of 12,722 microplastic particles per square metre daily in the city, a staggering 54 times higher than typical Beijing levels and 110 times greater than Guangzhou’s average.
A typhoon’s immense energy churns the ocean, suspending plastic particles from depths that are ejected into the atmosphere through sea spray. The storm then carries this invisible payload landward, where it is washed down upon urban centres and farmland in torrential rains.
For a city like Hong Kong, which faces multiple typhoons each year, this recurring exposure poses a significant public health challenge. The typhoon-driven surge consists overwhelmingly of small particles. This dramatic shift implies many of the particles are in the inhalable size range, which dramatically elevates community exposure, creating a persistent risk, especially for vulnerable groups like children, the elderly and those with respiratory conditions.
Most alarmingly, we have identified a dangerous climate feedback loop. Warmer oceans create stronger typhoons that redistribute more microplastics. Simultaneously, higher temperatures accelerate plastic fragmentation. Critically, these microplastics can disrupt the ocean’s ability to absorb carbon, potentially accelerating the warming that fuels the cycle.
Confronting this dual threat demands a coordinated response. Public health protocols must integrate microplastic monitoring into air quality reports and extreme weather advisories. Coastal authorities should prioritise pre-season beach clean-ups to limit the plastic available for storms to scatter.
Critically, this is a transboundary crisis. Since typhoons originate near the garbage patches of the Pacific – or zones in the ocean where marine debris, primarily plastic, accumulates due to swirling ocean currents known as gyres – a new regional cooperation framework is essential for nations in the typhoon corridor to collectively monitor, clean and reduce plastic waste.
The message is clear: the storms hitting our region will be both stronger and more polluted. While systemic change is crucial, we as consumers must also take the first step by drastically reducing our plastic use and ensuring proper disposal. By confronting climate change and plastic pollution together, we can still secure a safer future. The time for bold, collective action is now.
Dr Taiseer Hussain Nafea, School of Geographical Sciences, University of Nottingham Ningbo China
Promote anti-scam app to mainland students
The recent report on mainland Chinese students losing HK$110 million (US$14 million) to scams in the first nine months of this year underlines a gap in Hong Kong’s fraud prevention system. While the police are right to upgrade the Scameter+ app with artificial intelligence that can analyse screenshots and social‑media content, technological power alone will not stop scams if too few people use the tool.
From its launch in 2023 until April this year, Scameter+ had fewer than a million downloads, in a city of over seven million people. By contrast, mainland China has rolled out the National Anti-Fraud Centre app that many Chinese have installed. The app requires real-name registration and warns potential victims if suspicious content is found.
Mainland students in Hong Kong may assume similar safeguards exist here. To build trust and extend protection to all users, especially new arrivals, the police should publish regular usage and success data, and universities should include Scameter+ education in student orientations. Transparency and outreach will make Hong Kong safer.
Feiyang Situ and San Man Wong, Kowloon Tong
Compensation for helpers’ families must reach them
The deadly blaze of Wang Fuk Court on November 26 has traumatised Hong Kong. Among those who lost their lives were 10 domestic helpers, nine of them Indonesian and one Filipino. As of Thursday, 30 others are still unaccounted for.
There were 235 migrant helpers, mainly from Indonesia and the Philippines, working in the eight blocks of Wang Fuk Court. The blaze was first reported at 2.51pm when most of the domestic helpers would be at home before they went out to pick up the children from school or buy groceries for dinner. During the blaze, some helpers had stayed behind to take care of the old and young at home. Some unfortunately fell victim to the fire.
Hong Kong people owe a lot to migrant domestic helpers in the city, who cook and clean and take care of children and the elderly, allowing many women to go out and work.
The government has offered compensation and financial support to the affected workers: nearly HK$800,000 (US$102,700) for the family of each of the workers who died; HK$50,000 or HK$100,000 for each injured worker (depending on the length of their hospital stay) and HK$20,000 for each affected worker.
Efforts must be made to ensure that each worker or their family members receive the payout. In addition to monetary compensation, they also need consolation and spiritual support.
Albert Lau, Kwun Tong

data.title: AI tops the mind of this fifth-generation Illinois farmer in China-owned Syngenta’s mega IPO
data.description:
Jeff Rowe grinned with satisfaction when the dashboard of his John Deere combine harvester showed the yield of the first run on his family’s farm during this year’s harvest: 253 bushels per acre, equivalent to 13.8 tonnes of corn per hectare.
The yield on the 100-acre Schubert parcel was the highest in many years, said Rowe, the CEO of Syngenta Group and the son of the owners of Rowe Farm, Dean and Carol. Located in the corn belt of Illinois state in the US Midwest, the farm used Syngenta’s newly improved seeds, weed retardants and pest control solutions, drone scanning and artificial intelligence (AI) on its soybeans and corn crops, Rowe said.
“We are at a very exciting time in agriculture, where there is a lot of technological innovation,” Rowe said in the air-conditioned cockpit as he powered up the US$1 million harvester on the final day of September.
“For me, this is the best job there is; all the science and technological innovation at the disposal of farmers like my family” to help them get to the front of the agricultural revolution, he said.

Rowe’s full-time job is actually seven time zones away from the sparsely populated, wide open fields of middle America, in Basel, a city of 200,000 people straddling both banks of the River Rhine in Switzerland, where he runs a company with 56,000 employees and operations covering 90 countries.
After the year’s harvest wrapped up and the family celebrated Thanksgiving, Rowe turned his attention towards one of the biggest goals of his day job: the successful initial public offering (IPO) of Syngenta, widely expected to be one of the world’s largest fundraising exercises in 2026 when it lands next year in Hong Kong.
The company was in preliminary talks to banks and advisers, and the plan is heading in the right direction, according to sources familiar with the matter who declined to provide financial details.

Rowe said AI was a “transformative technology” for global agriculture because it helped farmers like him - a fifth-generation toiler of the land - make smarter decisions to get more out of their crops.
It is a subject he has spoken widely about, from agriculture shows in farming communities to boardrooms and to the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. AI will be front and centre of Syngenta’s pitch to investors as the company prepares its stock offer.
“It takes us about 10 years to bring a product to market, from discovery to commercialisation; my goal is to reduce that by 30 per cent” with AI, Rowe said.

An example of what Rowe called “chemistry by design” is Project Kato, a task force created to develop a new chemical subclass of herbicides called metproxybicyclone, scheduled for launch in Argentina in 2026.
Named after the crime-fighting sidekick of the fictional character Green Hornet, Kato grew out of an AI model. It predicted that grass weeds in Brazil and Argentina would develop resistance to the so-called third generation of herbicides that had been on the market for almost two decades. Farmers at first dismissed the warning, as they saw no signs of such weeds.
Syngenta stuck to its science. Two years after Kato went into development, farmers in several South American markets reported that their crop yields had fallen by 30 per cent due to a new breed of “superweeds” that no herbicide could thwart. The discovery turned into a boon for Syngenta, boosting the sales forecast of metproxybicyclone tenfold to US$1 billion, from the US$100 million in the original business case, Rowe said.
“It is an example of how AI can predict what farmers need, use it to design the product, and then get it to market faster,” he said, adding that every research project at Syngenta today uses machine learning models to identify novel active ingredients. “In my career, that is entirely new.”

Syngenta also uses AI to track millions of transactions worldwide “to maintain pricing discipline” and “optimise the value-price mix” to protect the value of its products as well as the return on investments for research and development, Rowe said. “That is going to unlock a tremendous amount of value,” he said.
The third part of the strategy is to improve the production modelling, demand forecasting and market prediction to cut down on wasteful excess production.
“This is where our Chinese footprint is so valuable because Chinese chemicals manufacturers are by far the most efficient and effective in the world. Syngenta has access to the best manufacturing efficiencies in the world in our industry,” he said.

Syngenta, wholly owned by the ChemChina subsidiary of China’s state-owned chemicals producer Sinochem, is the result of several mergers and acquisitions over a quarter of a century.
It was set up in 2000 in a merger of the agrichemical businesses of the pharmaceutical giants Novartis and AstraZeneca. In 2017, ChemChina bought Syngenta for US$43 billion in what still stands as the largest overseas takeover by a Chinese company.
Three years later, in 2020, the current Syngenta grew out of the merger of the agricultural businesses of ChemChina and Sinochem, with several brands within the previous Syngenta portfolio.
“Syngenta’s strategic importance to Sinochem is underpinned by its control over the parent’s entire agrochemical operation, which enjoys favourable growth prospects and provides a competitive advantage in both domestic and international markets,” Fitch Ratings said on November 5, as it affirmed Syngenta’s “A” credit rating with a stable outlook. “Syngenta makes substantial financial contributions to Sinochem, accounting for over 35 per cent of the parent’s total assets” and earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation (Ebitda), Fitch said.

Third-quarter Ebitda jumped 35 per cent to US$900 million, boosting Syngenta’s earnings for the first nine months by 31 per cent to US$3.4 billion, even though sales for the three quarters dipped by 1 per cent. That came on the heels of a 25 per cent gain in interim Ebitda.
The crop protection business – herbicides, insecticides, fungicides and seed treatments – was the biggest contributor to Syngenta’s top line, making up 47 per cent, or US$9.8 billion, of the company’s nine-month revenue.
Seeds contributed to 15.8 per cent of Syngenta’s nine-month sales, led by a 13 per cent jump in demand in Brazil and a 20 per cent increase across Latin America, as a worsening US-China trade war drove Chinese soybean buyers towards alternatives to American crops.

Syngenta’s pending IPO has taken a circuitous route to reach Hong Kong. When Rowe took the company’s top job in January 2024, its US$10 billion IPO plan was languishing on Shanghai’s Star board, a market that was hastily set up a year earlier to help China’s technology start-ups raise capital.
The capital market withered during China’s Covid-19 pandemic lockdown, sapping investors’ appetite for everything and anything related to China. To complicate matters, regulators batted over whether the specialist in agricultural science was considered a technology company that qualified for listing on the Star board.
That culminated in Rowe’s decision two months after his promotion to pull the plug on the IPO, a “hard choice” that he said was needed to avoid the “distraction” and redirect Syngenta’s focus towards growth. Since then, the company’s Ebitda has increased for three consecutive quarters, hitting all the financial milestones along the way, giving the board the confidence to revive its fundraising plan.

Rowe’s career at Syngenta began in 2016 when he reorganised its global seeds business and steered it back to profitability. He studied agricultural economics and molecular biology at Iowa State University and obtained a Global Executive MBA from the London School of Economics.
The Rowes’ farming tradition goes back to the days when Abraham Lincoln was still practising law in Illinois, before he entered politics. A county road that currently runs along the edge of one of the Rowe family’s soybean plots, designated Route 29 in 1918, was frequently travelled on by the young Lincoln, according to state archives.
Harvest time, which usually takes about six weeks every year, is always a family affair. Dean and Carol, both in their early 80s, still tend to the land.
Dean, 82, awakened before dawn to drive the combine harvester. Rowe, who turned 52 in May , returned from Basel to help with the annual harvest, joining conference calls with senior executives around the world in between runs on the harvester, which took less than a day to run through a 100-acre plot. He will do the same when the planting season begins in March.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Syngenta (@syngenta)



Rowe’s son Austin, a third-year agricultural science student at Iowa State, also helped. Even his nephew Elliott - who turned 15 on the first day of harvest - skipped a day of school to drive the grain collector.
While Rowe worked the corn field, Dean powered up a second harvester to run through the adjoining soybean field.
Agriculture is where big science, big tech and AI come together, and Rowe is turning his company and his parents’ farm into the role model.
Dean and Carol get live data from their combine harvesters that indicate the crop yield via a smartphone app developed by the machine’s assembler, John Deere. They look at real-time corn and soybean futures prices on the Chicago Mercantile Exchange.
They use a liquid biostimulant called YieldOn, produced by Syngenta from the extracts of seaweed harvested off the Norwegian coast, to increase raw crop productivity. Rowe even engaged a company to scan his fields with DJI drones to identify plots with pests and weeds.
“The technology gives farmers the tools to identify problems in their fields in real time,” said Jason Minton, the chief commercial officer of Taranis, the crop scanner.

The soybeans and corn from the Rowe farm were hauled by truck to a granary along the Illinois River, where they were consolidated for exports. The Rowe farm, which covers 2,500 acres, is profitable because the family owns their land. The same cannot be said of farmers who lease their land, as they are being squeezed by falling commodity prices and rising costs.
China, formerly the biggest customer of US soybeans, used to buy 60 per cent of the grains shipped from Illinois. The Chinese have shifted their purchases to Brazil in a tit-for-tat retaliation to President Donald Trump’s trade war.
After the Chinese and US presidents met in South Korea in October, China agreed to buy at least 12 million tonnes of American soybeans in November and December, with the pledge to import at least 25 million tonnes every year between 2026 and 2028, the White House said.
Without the detente, soybean exports to China could tumble 52 per cent, or by between 14 and 16 million tonnes per year, according to an October 2024 study by the US National Corn Growers’ Association. Corn shipments could plunge by 84 per cent to 2.2 million tonnes per annum, the study said.
Still, the slings and arrows of geopolitics and the trade spat were only abstractions that hardly register on the family’s daily lives, Carol said.
“We almost never talk about it at the dinner table”, she said. “We pay a lot of attention to the swings in prices [of soy beans and corn], and talk about how we can keep costs down.”

data.title: A Chinese home-grown business jet is on the way. Will it rival Gulfstream?
data.description:
China’s top aircraft maker is stepping up efforts to promote its first business jet as it looks to break into a market dominated by Western aviation firms like Gulfstream Aerospace.
The Commercial Aircraft Corporation of China (Comac) took the rare step of showcasing a completed version of the model – called the Comac Business Jet, or CBJ – at an exhibition in the southern Chinese city of Guangzhou in October.
The move signalled the state-owned company was “seeking to break into the large-cabin VIP aircraft market” with the CBJ, aviation industry news outlet AIN Media Group reported on Thursday.
The CBJ is adapted from Comac’s C909 commercial passenger jet, which is already used by airlines in China and several other countries. The business jet version can reportedly seat as many as 29 passengers.
It first received regulatory approval a decade ago, and the Civil Aviation Administration of China issued the twin-jet’s type certificate validation in 2021, according to AIN.
The Chinese model is intended eventually to compete with Gulfstream – the iconic American company that made the world’s first purpose-built business aircraft in 1958 – along with Airbus, Boeing, Bombardier and other players in the crowded private aircraft market.
“They might have made a decision to compete in the same sector,” said David Dixon, the Hong Kong-based president of business aircraft seller Jetcraft Asia, adding that aircraft firms often used exhibitions to gauge the market.
“Everyone else has done it, so it wouldn’t surprise me.”


But the CBJ had only reached the prototype stage so far and had no known timeline for release, with Comac currently focusing on “public users”, said Li Hanming, an independent civil aviation analyst.
Shanghai-based Comac is already vying with Airbus and Boeing with its C919 narrowbody commercial aircraft. The C919 began flying commercially in 2023 and Comac has secured hundreds of orders for the jet from Chinese airlines.
The CBJ has been “validated” to operate at high-altitude airfields such as Daocheng Yading Airport in China’s southwestern Sichuan province, which is located 4,411 metres (14,472 feet) above sea level, according to AIN.
Business jet production could take years as each plane was tailor-made for a specific client based on how they wanted the interior configured, Dixon said.
China’s civil aviation authorities are trying to develop more infrastructure for private jets. In 2018, officials said the country needed to build 216 new civil airports by 2035, the state-run Xinhua News Agency reported.
But China still lacked the business jet-friendly secondary airports available in other parts of the world, meaning the country’s private jet industry could “take time to develop”, Dixon said.
Chinese private jet owners typically use the vehicles for a range of company affairs, from travelling to overseas meetings in Africa and South America to visiting facilities in remote parts of western China.
Comac did not respond to a request for comment on Friday.

data.title: The Thai influencer bringing viral warfare to Cambodia’s border
data.description:
When Malaysia brokered a ceasefire between Thailand and Cambodia in July, many hoped the guns would finally fall silent along the border. Instead, new noises have filled the front lines: the hum of drones, the whirr of smartphones and, in one surreal moment, the wail of “ghost sounds” blasted across the Cambodian side.
The source of these sonic bursts was not a military psychological operations unit, but someone who goes by “Gun Jompalang” – possibly Thailand’s most controversial self-styled social media influencer, whose name roughly translates as “Gun the Superpower”.
In an age when social media often shapes public sentiment faster than any press release, influencers have become powerful intermediaries between the Thai state, soldiers and people’s screens.
Gun’s rise from online crusader to battlefield personality reflects a striking shift in how wars are waged and witnessed. Calling himself a “justice crusader” with 9 million followers on Facebook alone, Gun is apparently followed for his patriotic stunts and “justice missions”.
He has made himself a fixture along the Thai-Cambodian border since the latest conflict broke out this summer.

Gun’s popularity rests on his direct, performative patriotism. Through live streams, short videos and Facebook posts, he has chronicled at least 30 visits to different border towns, delivering supplies and tactical gear to frontline troops, reportedly paid for by public donations.
His supporters praise him for acting more quickly than the Thai military, as the military is subject to a slow procurement process. In a sense, Gun fills a logistical gap, but this “efficiency” is what makes him controversial – he operates outside any chain of command and is answerable to his followers but not to the laws of war.
His presence on the border marks a disquieting evolution in how modern conflicts can unfold. In the past, battle front lines were the domain of soldiers and authorised journalists, but today, they have sometimes become stages for “influencers” whose actions blur the boundaries between humanitarianism, nationalism and spectacle.
Their involvement in crisis or conflict zones poses urgent legal and ethical questions: when a so-called influencer crosses into a conflict zone, are they doing so as a private citizen (of any warring party), a media outlet or an unauthorised combatant?
Gun has trodden a thin line between humanitarian assistance and the militarisation of his influence. When he allegedly used loudspeakers on October 12 to project “ghost sounds” into Cambodian villages, it was more than a prank. His act arguably crossed into the territory of psyops (psychological operations), tactics that militaries use to scare civilians or demoralise troops on the enemy’s side.

International humanitarian law strictly prohibits the targeting of civilians: Cambodia’s Human Rights Commission, NGOs and Thai human rights defenders have condemned Gun’s act as intimidatory, noting that his broadcasts spread panic among internally displaced people sheltering near the border.
This episode exposes a deeper governance and applicability gap in the international law surrounding contemporary warfare. Traditional international legal frameworks, such as those enshrined in the Geneva Conventions, assume that states and their militaries are the primary actors in war.
However, influencers like Gun occupy an unclear legal and ethical space since they are neither soldiers nor journalists, nor even formally contracted private actors. These influencers’ participation in conflict spaces does not fit neatly within existing frameworks such as the Montreux Document on regulating private security contractors or the Tallinn Manual on cyberwarfare.
This ambiguity raises thorny questions about attribution and responsibility. If an individual, such as an influencer, supplies troops, disseminates propaganda or conducts psyops, the question of attribution arises.
Could their conduct be linked to the state, thereby triggering state responsibility under international law? Could the individual be considered a direct participant in hostilities and thus forfeit the protection traditionally offered to civilians in wartime?
These are not theoretical puzzles but the emerging realities of digitally mediated conflict, in which the existing provisions or customary rules of international law are silent or insufficient.
What makes this new frontier especially perilous is the logic of the attention economy used by social media giants. The more dramatic the footage from an influencer, the greater the engagement. By extension, revenue follows. In such an ecosystem, patriotism becomes performance.
Gun’s supporters argue that he boosts morale and provides material support when the state cannot. In a sense, he performs a civic function by raising awareness, galvanising domestic (Thai) audiences, mobilising resources and humanising soldiers.

However, critics point out that Gun’s viral videos risk inflaming Thai nationalist sentiment and hardening public attitudes at a delicate diplomatic moment. His acts may risk undermining the ceasefire agreement and expose more civilians to harm.
What can be done? First, the Thai government should clarify its regulations governing civilian participation in defence operations, including transparency requirements for any individuals crowdfunding and donating to the armed forces.
Second, social media firms should enforce stricter vetting and more robust content moderation in conflict contexts. The deployment of brief bans or social media “blackouts” in contentious times that were ubiquitous during the recent anti-government protests in Nepal and Indonesia, for instance, were arguably political cop-outs, shielding these platforms from problems with their host governments and doing the due diligence of content moderation and risk monitoring.
Finally, the Association of Southeast Asian Nations can begin drafting a regional code of conduct for digital actors in crisis settings to acknowledge that the influencer economy is now part of the conflict landscape.
Gun Jompalang may see himself as a Thai patriot, not a provocateur. But his fame has crossed into a real conflict zone and he might have crossed the line between helping and harming. If Thai and regional policymakers fail to quickly address this grey zone, the next ceasefire may not be broken by gunfire, but by a live stream.
Surachanee Sriyai is a Visiting Fellow with the Media, Technology and Society Programme at ISEAS – Yusof Ishak Institute. This article was first published by ISEAS – Yusof Ishak Institute’s commentary website fulcrum.sg.

data.title: Chernobyl safety shield damaged, UN confirms. Ukraine blames Russian drones
data.description:
A protective shield at the Chernobyl nuclear plant in war-torn Ukraine, built to contain radioactive material from the 1986 disaster, can no longer perform its main safety function due to drone damage, the UN nuclear watchdog said on Friday, a strike Ukraine has attributed to Russia.
The International Atomic Energy Agency said an inspection last week of the steel confinement structure completed in 2019 found the drone impact in February, three years into Russia’s conflict in Ukraine, had degraded the structure.
IAEA Director General Rafael Grossi said in a statement the inspection “mission confirmed that the [protective structure] had lost its primary safety functions, including the confinement capability, but also found that there was no permanent damage to its load-bearing structures or monitoring systems”.

Grossi said repairs had already been carried out “but comprehensive restoration remains essential to prevent further degradation and ensure long-term nuclear safety”.
The UN reported on February 14 that Ukrainian authorities said a drone with a high explosive warhead struck the plant, caused a fire and damaged the protective cladding around reactor No 4, which was destroyed in the 1986 disaster.
Ukrainian authorities said the drone was Russian. Moscow denied it had attacked the plant.
Radiation levels remained normal and stable and there were no reports of radiation leaks, the UN said in February.


The 1986 Chernobyl explosion sent radiation across Europe and prompted Soviet authorities to mobilise vast numbers of men and equipment to deal with the accident. The plant’s last working reactor was closed in 2000.
Russia occupied the plant and the surrounding area for more than a month in the first weeks of its February 2022 invasion of Ukraine as its forces initially tried to advance on the Ukrainian capital, Kyiv.
The IAEA had conducted the inspection at the same time as a countrywide survey of damage to electricity substations by the nearly four-year war between Ukraine and Russia.

data.title: China’s official media calls for end to name-and-shame campaign against grass-roots cadres
data.description:
China’s official media has called on disciplinary watchdogs to stop giving low-level officials insulting nicknames that imply they are not doing their jobs properly.
The provincial publicity department of the eastern province of Zhejiang became the latest to weigh in on this issue. In a commentary posted on its official social media on Tuesday, it warned that this approach was likely to backfire.
It criticised the use of labels such as “refrigerator” officials to describe those who “don’t speak up, stay cold and don’t change”; “treadmill” types who “look busy and sweaty but who make no progress in their work”; or “lipstick” cadres who make things pretty on the surface without doing the in-depth work.
Others were accused of “lying flat” – a popular Chinese term to describe those who make minimal efforts – or “lying on their side”, referring to those who only act when given instructions.
The article said this “label-based evaluation … smacked of sensationalism” and warned that it was a barrier to properly understanding the dilemmas and problems grass-roots officials faced.
For example, it said those accused of lying flat might not necessarily be lazy but could be suffering from physical and mental exhaustion as a result of an unfair workload. Alternatively, it said, they might be worried that officials who took the initiative were at greater risk of making mistakes and being punished as a result.
It also expressed sympathy for those accused of lying on their side, saying they might face an overly strict authorisation process or have “nitpicking” superiors.
Beijing Daily, the capital’s official newspaper, published a similar commentary in early November, also calling for a halt to the use of such labels.
The current campaign began with another commentary published in August in China Organisation and Personnel Works News, a newspaper controlled by the Communist Party’s top personnel department.


The article warned against relying on name-and-shame tactics and said it was better to get to the bottom of cadres’ problems and offer them the help and support they needed.
Such tactics are in line with a drive from Beijing to tighten political control over the grass-roots, but the central authorities are trying to balance this with concerns that officials are struggling with poor morale and layers of bureaucracy.
Some local authorities have handed out “awards” to officials deemed to be performing poorly, sometimes publicly shaming them as well.
For example, a Sichuan television programme went viral at the end of last year when it showed several grass-roots officials from the city of Suining being forced to pose for photos with certificates branding them as “snails”, “hedgehogs” or “ostriches”.
Snails are seen as too slow to respond, hedgehogs as prickly and difficult to approach – in this case housing officials had been blamed for rejecting applications repeatedly – while the ostriches were accused of putting their heads in the sand and failing to show initiative.
Other cities, including Taizhou in eastern Jiangsu province and Zunyi in southwestern Guizhou, have handed out similar awards to officials deemed to have underperformed, ignored residents’ interests or failed to correct mistakes. Their annual performance bonuses were also cut.
But such campaigns have backfired by further harming morale, according to Alfred Wu, an associate professor at the National University of Singapore’s Lee Kuan Yew School of Public Policy.
“Many of these problems are caused by the further centralisation of decision-making power to the higher hierarchies, which leave the grass roots very limited room to make their own discretionary decisions,” Wu said.
Beijing has realised that there is a problem with morale and motivation and has taken a series of measures targeting the needless meetings low-level officials are forced to attend and the unnecessary reports they are expected to write.
In August, the party’s General Office and the State Council issued regulations to reduce red tape, streamline workflows and improve efficiency to ensure officials could devote more time to practical work.
The rules mandated strict control over the number of documents generated and meetings held, with content required to be concise and to the point. They also said virtual meetings should be favoured over in-person gatherings.

data.title: Updated Taiwan safety guide sparks fierce debate amid Beijing’s mounting military pressure
data.description:
Taiwan’s updated crisis and war survival guide has ignited fierce political debate, hailed by officials as a lifeline amid Beijing’s mounting military pressure but blasted by critics as costly propaganda prompting fear rather than readiness.
Mass distribution of In Case of Crisis: Taiwan’s National Public Safety Guide – quickly dubbed the “little orange book” – began late last month, targeting 9.83 million households.
The 29-page comic-style publication explains how to stockpile food and water, locate shelters and identify signs of escalating threats to the public, ranging from cyberattacks to a full-scale military assault.
It updates previous guidance by including information on landslide-dammed lakes following recent weather disasters, urging citizens to monitor warnings, heed evacuation orders and work with local authorities.
The foreword now carries Taiwanese leader William Lai Ching-te’s signature, a move the defence ministry said added “authority and credibility” and encouraged public trust.
Aside from offering guidance for natural disasters and extreme weather, the guide cites mainland China’s military threat as a direct challenge.

Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary and has ramped up pressure on Taiwan since Lai took office last year, having labelled him a separatist.
Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
The handbook details steps for finding air-raid shelters across Taiwan through mobile apps and preparing emergency kits, even for pets.
In addition, it highlights potential precursors to armed conflict such as sabotage of undersea cables, cyberwarfare and hostile forces patrolling around the island and demanding to board and inspect Taiwanese vessels.
It warns that “foreign hostile actors” could deploy deepfakes and disinformation to weaken public morale.
The handbook has added cybersecurity alerts on products made in mainland China, such as cameras and sensors. It singled out DeepSeek, WeChat, TikTok, Douyin and RedNote as potential risks for compromising personal data.
Perhaps most striking is one message printed in bold text: “If Taiwan comes under military invasion, any information about defeat or government surrender is fake news.”
Other new sections include advice for parents on discussing war with children and a chapter encouraging citizens to join civil-defence teams or even enlist as volunteers.

Officials said the update reflected a broader push for “whole-of-society resilience” as mainland Chinese military activity intensifies in the Taiwan Strait.
Lin Fei-fan, deputy secretary general of the island’s National Security Council, said “preparedness is the only way to deter aggression” and that “we are not provoking war” with the handbook.
Lin and other military officials have justified the large-scale distribution of copies by drawing comparisons with Nordic states such as Sweden and Finland and the Baltic country Estonia. All three have mass-mailed wartime survival manuals in recent years.
The officials said Tokyo regularly distributed disaster handbooks to millions of households and argued Taiwan was now acting “consistent with leading democratic nations”.
But one opinion poll suggested the roll-out had some way to go to win over the public.
A survey of more than 25,000 people, conducted on Wednesday by the app platform Line Today, revealed a significant split: 40 per cent of respondents felt the guide had “no practical effect”, compared with only 22 per cent who described it as “highly helpful”.
Smaller numbers of respondents called the handbook limited in usefulness or said they had yet to receive it.
Photos circulating online showed unopened hard copies tossed into recycling bins. One sarcastic comment read: “When missiles hit, scan the QR code, register your account – maybe win a lucky draw?”
Some in the online community voiced privacy concerns, citing past breaches in Taiwan’s government database.
A number of users said the booklet should have been digital rather than printed, though others countered that computer networks could be disabled in wartime.

At Neihu Technology Park in Taipei, workers told a local television channel they had never seen the booklet. After taking a brief look, one called it a “waste of taxpayer money”.
“Taiwan is tiny,” another said. “One artillery shell and we’re done. Where are we supposed to hide?”
Liu Shih-fang, Taiwan’s interior minister, slammed those who dismissed the handbook, saying such people must have “prince or princess syndrome”. Her comments sparked backlash.
Told of Liu’s rebuke, one person told a television reporter: “We’re overworked labourers, not pampered royalty. Stop spreading fear.”
Opposition lawmakers have seized on the guide’s mixed reception, accusing the ruling independence-leaning Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) of spending NT$65 million (US$2 million) on a vanity project.
The printing costs were covered by the cabinet’s emergency reserve fund – normally earmarked for natural disasters – instead of regular defence appropriations.
Wang Hung-wei, a legislator with the main opposition party Kuomintang (KMT), asked: “Is Taiwan preparing for war next year or simply burning cash before year-end?”
Fellow KMT lawmaker Hsu Chiao-hsin said it was “overly complicated to access shelter data”, for it required “scanning QR codes, downloading apps and registering accounts”.
Others complained that the handbook’s maps were unclear and that many actual shelters, including basements in government buildings, were not listed.

Lawmakers from the Taiwan People’s Party (TPP) likewise raised objections to the guide.
“Distributing it like an election flier creates waste and burdens local chiefs,” said TPP legislator Lin Kuo-cheng. “Why insist on paper when government pushes digitalisation?”
Fellow TPP legislator Mai Yu-chen echoed Lin’s criticism, saying “a survival manual must be usable, not a slogan book”.
But Lin of Taiwan’s National Security Council and military officials defended the decision, saying printed copies “ensure seniors and low-tech households are covered if power or networks fail”.
They insisted the handbook was just “one element of a wider resilience strategy” that included a multibillion-dollar budget to boost strategic stockpiles and strengthen infrastructure against blockade risks.
In the guide’s preface, Lai, Taiwan’s leader, wrote: “Our safety hinges on our will to defend ourselves and our readiness before crises occur.”
Meanwhile, the island’s defence ministry denied any intention to bypass legislative scrutiny or politicise the initiative.
Security analysts said the controversy reflected a broader societal divide over how to confront the possibility of armed conflict.
Former US Marine Michael Hunzeker, an associate professor at George Mason University in Virginia, praised the booklet’s clarity but argued its message was too soft.
“There’s almost no mention of war,” he told Taiwan’s Central News Agency.


“If I have one concrete recommendation, I do think the manual could be more forthright with the Taiwanese people, more transparent and honest with them about the nature of the military threat,” Hunzeker said.
Citizens needed psychological preparation for a worst-case scenario, he explained, warning that underestimating the risk could leave people paralysed in a crisis.
James Yi-fan Chen, a diplomacy professor at Tamkang University in New Taipei City, said the publication was in part “political propaganda at home and abroad”.
Chen believed the guide would be valuable in the event that digital access was lost but that it still lacked essential actionable information.
“Instead of simply distributing these booklets, Taiwanese citizens may need actual rehearsals to experience warlike scenarios,” he said.
“Otherwise, the guidebook may only heighten anxiety without giving people clear instructions on what to do.”
Lin of Taiwan’s National Security Council maintained that democracies had a responsibility to tell citizens how to survive disasters and conflict.
Amid increasingly sour cross-strait relations, local supporters of the “little orange book” said it was a necessary first step to prepare civilians for crises that now seemed less hypothetical.
For some observers and sceptics, however, it amounted to a hollow gesture serving political priorities more than practical defence and hinted at a DPP administration overstating the war threat to justify spending.
